refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall support teleoperation with end-to-end communication latency that ensures safe remote operation.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is mostly atomic, focusing on teleoperation latency compatible with safety. It is solution-neutral, specifying what the system must achieve (latency compatible with safe operation) without prescribing how. The phrase 'compatible with safe remote operation' ties latency to safety requirements, which is appropriate for the problem domain. No multiple obligations or implementation details are present.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure the end-to-end communication latency during teleoperation under agreed network conditions and verify that the 95th percentile latency (p95) is less than or equal to 150 ms, ensuring compliance with safety standards and operational policies.
sub_frs: The NGHE solution shall support teleoperation with end-to-end communication latency less than or equal to 150 ms at the 95th percentile under agreed network conditions.

candidate_dps: Implement a communication protocol optimized for low-latency end-to-end data transfer
Design a system architecture that prioritizes real-time data processing and minimal buffering
Incorporate adaptive latency monitoring and dynamic adjustment mechanisms
Separate control and feedback channels to minimize interference and delays
Use predictive control algorithms to compensate for communication delays
selected_dp.selected_dp: Implement a communication protocol optimized for low-latency end-to-end data transfer

potential_sources: Tight integration between communication protocol and control algorithms could cause coupling
Shared resources in network stack might introduce dependencies
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP focuses on the communication protocol as a distinct design parameter, which supports independence from control logic and other system functions, minimizing coupling.

assumptions_risks: Network conditions remain within agreed parameters to meet latency requirements
Latency measurement and monitoring are accurate and timely
Operational policies do not impose additional delays or constraints that affect latency
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Consider explicitly including a DP for adaptive latency monitoring and dynamic adjustment to handle variable network conditions and ensure consistent latency performance.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Module responsible for remote control and communication between operator and vehicle

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Bi-directional data flow for vehicle status and control feedback

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Commands and instructions from operator to vehicle

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network connection used for communication protocol

module_containment_scores.notes: Very broad module; contains many unrelated functions, leading to high diffusion of communication protocol logic.
Vehicle module includes hardware and embedded systems; communication protocol partially contained but some logic diffuses to Teleoperation and Network Link.
Most specific module focused on remote control and communication; communication protocol fits well with minimal diffusion.
Safety Services focus on alerts and safety monitoring; communication protocol is peripheral and would cause high diffusion.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the most appropriate boundary for implementing the low-latency communication protocol because it directly manages remote control and data exchange, minimizing diffusion and maximizing cohesion.
containment_risks: Potential dependency on underlying Network Link interface may cause some coupling outside Teleoperation.
If communication protocol requires vehicle-side implementation, partial diffusion to Vehicle module may occur.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: If diffusion to Vehicle module is significant, consider refining the DP to separate protocol logic into distinct layers: one for Teleoperation-side communication management and another for Vehicle-side protocol adaptation, improving containment clarity.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The DP directly addresses the FR by specifying a communication protocol optimized for low-latency end-to-end data transfer, which aligns with the requirement to support teleoperation with latency ensuring safe remote operation (p95 ≤ 150 ms). This mapping is clear and verifiable against the latency acceptance criterion.

diffusion_risks: Communication protocol logic is partially diffused between Teleoperation and Vehicle modules, and some coupling exists with the Network Link interface.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: The Teleoperation module has the highest containment score (9) and lowest diffusion score (2), indicating it is the best architectural boundary for implementing the communication protocol. However, some diffusion to Vehicle and Network Link modules is noted, which poses a risk to modularity and maintainability.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of the FR through the IM is feasible by testing the Teleoperation module's communication latency under agreed network conditions, as it encapsulates the protocol logic and interfaces with telemetry and network link.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of the DP to the Teleoperation IM is clear and appropriate, given the module's scope and ownership candidate status, though partial diffusion to Vehicle module should be monitored.

recommended_revisions: Refine the DP to separate protocol logic into distinct layers: one for Teleoperation-side communication management and another for Vehicle-side protocol adaptation to reduce diffusion and improve containment clarity.
Monitor and manage dependencies on the Network Link interface to minimize coupling outside the Teleoperation module.
If diffusion to the Vehicle module remains significant, consider defining explicit interfaces and responsibilities to isolate protocol adaptation logic within Vehicle, preserving modularity.

decision.justification: Although the gates passed indicating the DP and IM adequately address the FR, the noted diffusion of communication protocol logic between Teleoperation and Vehicle modules, and coupling with the Network Link interface, poses modularity and maintainability risks. Given the high safety criticality, it is prudent to revise the current level to refine the DP and IM allocation to reduce diffusion and clarify module boundaries before proceeding to further decomposition.

revise.why: To separate protocol logic into distinct layers for Teleoperation-side communication management and Vehicle-side protocol adaptation, reducing diffusion and improving containment clarity, and to manage dependencies on the Network Link interface to minimize coupling.

assumptions: The current DP sufficiently meets the latency requirement for teleoperation.
The Teleoperation module is the best candidate for implementing the communication protocol logic.
open_risks: Diffusion of protocol logic between Teleoperation and Vehicle modules may reduce modularity and maintainability.
Coupling with Network Link interface may introduce integration complexity.
evidence_needed: Verification results of refined DP layers showing reduced diffusion.
Architectural analysis demonstrating improved containment and modularity after revision.
